title: In the first quarter of 2026, investors poured roughly $242 billion into AI startups—about 80% of all venture capital deployed worldwide, according to Crunchbase. Nothing comparable appears in its historical data: never before had one sector absorbed such a large share of global startup investment in a single quarter.
blurb: Investors poured roughly $242B into AI startups in Q1 2026, about 80% of all global venture funding, according to Crunchbase. OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and Waymo alone took $188B of that, meaning a handful of large American companies absorbed 65% of the world's startup capital in a single quarter.

title: How a Gang of Thieves Pulled Off a Multimillion-Dollar Data Center Heist
blurb: A New York Times Magazine feature reconstructs how a crew stole millions of dollars in hardware from a data center, exposing how physical security lags the value now concentrated inside these anonymous buildings. The piece is a reminder that the industry's most valuable assets sit on racks that can be carried out a loading dock, not just breached over a network.

title: A dock that wakes up reliably
blurb: After years of Thunderbolt docks failing to reliably wake a docked laptop, the author found the problem vanished only after swapping monitors, not docks. Intel's Thunderbolt 4 spec formally required wake-from-sleep in its conformance suite, yet CalDigit's TS3 and TS4 docks still failed sporadically until an ASUS ROG Swift replaced an aging BenQ, suggesting the real culprit was monitor firmware all along.

title: In the first quarter of 2026, investors poured roughly $242 billion into AI startups—about 80% of all venture capital deployed worldwide, according to Crunchbase. Nothing comparable appears in its historical data: never before had one sector absorbed such a large share of global startup investment in a single quarter.
blurb: AI startups absorbed roughly $242B in Q1 2026, about 80% of all venture capital deployed worldwide, according to Crunchbase, an all-time high with no precedent in its records. The concentration matters more than the total, because OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI, and Waymo alone took $188B, or about 65% of global venture funding in the quarter. AI's share climbed from 55% a year earlier, so the story isn't more startups getting funded but a handful of large American companies becoming the market that everyone else is now competing for capital against.

title: FFmpeg chromakey Filter: Remove Green Screen Backgrounds
blurb: FFmpeg's chromakey filter keys out green screen backgrounds in YUV space, tuned via similarity and blend, with output to alpha-capable WebM or MOV.

title: A dock that wakes up reliably
blurb: After years of Thunderbolt docks (CalDigit TS3 and TS4) sporadically failing to wake a docked MacBook Pro M1 Max and ThinkPad X1 Carbon, the author found the problem vanished only after swapping the monitor to an ASUS ROG Swift PG27UCDM. The post documents the full working configuration and concedes the root cause is unknown, likely buggy display firmware or a wake-timeout in the old BenQ, with wake now consistently under a second.

title: Compiling PHP DTOs: from reflection to 4.5M hydrations per second
blurb: Simple Data Objects compiles a specialized closure per PHP data class, turning attribute-driven DTO hydration into inline array reads with no reflection, attribute parsing, or generic dispatch on the hot path. The generated code is persisted to an opcache-served cache and PHP 8.4 lazy ghosts defer hydration until first property access, reaching 4.5M hydrations per second.

title: Lowering your DNS TTL will not speed up your failover, and here is the data showing why
blurb: Testing multi-cloud DNS failover across nine kills and three TTL settings, the author found detection time held constant at ~48 seconds regardless of TTL, because Route 53's health-check polling and quorum logic govern it, not the record's TTL. Lowering TTL only affects propagation, which turned out to be resolver-dependent: at TTL=300 Cloudflare's resolver oscillated for over 325 seconds after failover while Google DNS stabilized far faster.

title: Lowering your DNS TTL will not speed up your failover, and here is the data showing why
blurb: A final-year dissertation measured multi-cloud DNS failover across nine kills and three TTL settings, and found that lowering TTL barely moves your RTO. Failover splits into detection time, a near-constant ~48s governed by Route 53's health-check interval and failure threshold rather than TTL, and propagation time, which is resolver-dependent: at TTL=300 Cloudflare's resolver oscillated for over 325s while Google DNS stabilized quickly. The takeaway for on-call teams is to tune health-check intervals and thresholds, not just crank TTL down.
